title: Medical Examiner Rules Renee Good's Death a Homicide; Evidence Withheld by Feds
excerpt: The Hennepin County Medical Examiner officially classified the death of Renee Good — shot by ICE agent Jonathan Ross on January 7 — as a homicide caused by multiple gunshot wounds, while state investigators reported that the federal government had seized key evidence.
content:
On January 23, 2026, the Hennepin County Medical Examiner released its report classifying Renee Good's death as a homicide. The cause of death was listed as 'multiple gunshot wounds' after she was 'shot by a law enforcement officer.' A homicide classification is a medical determination that does not inherently indicate criminal wrongdoing.

County Attorney Mary Moriarty stated the federal government took Good's car, ICE agent Jonathan Ross's gun, and shell casings from the scene — leaving state investigators with severely limited evidence. An independent autopsy commissioned by Good's family indicated she was struck by at least three bullets, with the most serious wound entering near her left temple.

  • The independent autopsy found gunshot wounds to Good's left forearm, right breast, and head.
  • U.S. Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche said there was 'no basis for an investigation' at the federal level.
  • Minnesota officials described the federal government as actively preventing state investigation.
  • Questions about agent training — including when Ross was trained, what he was trained to do, and what is in his personnel file — were cited by the county attorney as central to any potential prosecution.
mediaPlaceholder: Hennepin County Medical Examiner official report listing cause and manner of Renee Good's death
